response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I find it quite worrying that there is so much fake news and misinformation floating around online these days, especially since I use social media only occasionally but still encounter this stuff. As a man in my 70s living in Milan with four grown kids and being part of the Italian community, I've always valued stability and truth from trusted sources. My anxiety makes me extra cautious about what's real, so when I see something suspicious on social media—like those viral posts that claim certain foods cause cancer or political figures saying outrageous things—I get uneasy right away. It feels like a gamble clicking links; you might land in trouble if it's not accurate.

Because my education was vocational and I worked as a delivery driver all these years, I'm practical by nature but sometimes overly so when it comes to trusting what people say online. My high neuroticism means I worry about the consequences of believing false things—could lead me astray or even make me look foolish in front of others—but being anxious also keeps me from engaging too much for fear of error.

One thing that helps is checking multiple sources, like reading Italian news sites because I'm native-born and know my language well enough to spot inconsistencies. But honestly? It's exhausting trying to verify everything when you're just casually browsing once or twice a week during your free time from driving deliveries. My hobbies don't help much either—birdwatching is more about observing reality than questioning it—but maybe that focus on detail makes me better at spotting lies elsewhere.

I think young people who are constantly online need some guidance, like my kids might give me sometimes when they point out something unreliable with their tech-savviness. But for someone older and less familiar with digital trends, the best approach is probably to be skeptical unless it comes from highly reputable places.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Step-by-step derivation: First, I considered the user's query about managing fake content on social media based on my own persona details—being elderly (74), having a high anxiety level, and being somewhat less tech-savvy due to low extraversion and agreeableness scores. Then, drawing from my background as a delivery driver with vocational education living in Milan, I reflected how practicality versus caution aligns here. Since fake content can be emotionally unsettling for anxious individuals but also dangerous if acted upon without verification, I focused on personal mitigation strategies that leverage existing traits like detail-orientation (from birdwatching) and critical thinking skills gained from lifelong work.
response_parsed.tone: Cautious yet concerned, with a touch of skepticism due to high neuroticism
